content.text: Severity and Impact Matrix for Claude Incidents

A well-calibrated severity model is the control surface for everything else in incident management: who gets paged, how fast, what war-room bridge is opened, whether a status page update goes out. Miscalibration in either direction is expensive — over-paging burns on-call engineers, under-paging delays customer-impacting fixes.

Claude incidents require a custom severity model because the failure modes differ from traditional software services. A 2 % error rate on a stateless HTTP API is usually tolerable; a 2 % rate of safety-policy violations on a language model is an immediate P0 regardless of overall throughput numbers.

attrs.alt: Claude Incident Severity Matrix: P0-P4 thresholds

content.text: Why Claude Incidents Need Their Own Severity Rubric
Standard SRE severity frameworks are built around availability and latency. Claude adds three dimensions that have no equivalent in a stateless compute service:
A response that violates Anthropic's usage policy or bypasses a content guardrail is a P0 incident regardless of error rate. One instance is one too many.
Claude may return 200 OK but produce incoherent or factually inverted responses due to a model serving issue or corrupted context window. Error-rate dashboards miss this entirely.
If a bug causes conversation context from one session to bleed into another, that is a P0 privacy incident even if the affected volume is tiny.
Safety and privacy incidents always override quantitative thresholds. A single confirmed safety violation or cross-session leak escalates to P0 immediately.
Complete outage, safety/trust violation, or cross-session data leak
API returning 5xx for > 25 % of requests; confirmed guardrail bypass; session contamination
Significant degradation affecting a meaningful customer segment; potential safety concern
Error rate 5-25 %; latency 2-5x baseline; guardrail near-miss spike
Limited degradation; single-digit customer impact; no safety or privacy dimension
Error rate 1-5 %; P95 latency elevated; streaming drops on a subset of regions
Minor degradation; < 1 % customers; quality issue without safety implication
Occasional empty completions for a specific model variant; slow response on a single AZ
No customer impact; cosmetic bug; investigation needed but no SLA urgency
Dashboard anomaly with no customer complaints; missing log label
Each dimension is measured independently. The highest severity triggered by
single dimension determines the incident's overall severity — dimensions do not average.
Confirmed policy violation or near-miss spike
Cross-session context bleed or PII exposure
Automated quality score drop vs baseline
Apply these steps in order when an alert fires or a report arrives. Stop at the first step that yields a severity.
Check for safety or privacy flags first.

Query the safety pipeline and the privacy-incident webhook. If either reports a confirmed event, severity is P0 immediately. Open the P0 bridge and page all-hands before gathering quantitative metrics — time to containment matters more than a complete picture.
Pull the 5-minute error-rate query from Prometheus (see code below). Apply the threshold table: > 25 % is P0, > 5 % is P1, > 1 % is P2, > 0.1 % is P3.
Divide the current P50 latency by the 7-day rolling baseline for the same clock hour. Use the ratio thresholds: > 5x is P0, > 2x is P1, > 1.5x is P2.
values in the error stream and divide by total active organisations. > 20 % is P0, > 5 % is P1, > 1 % is P2.
The incident severity is the minimum (most severe) value across all dimensions with a signal. A P2 error rate combined with a P0 safety flag is a P0 incident.
Write the observed metric values and the dimension that drove the severity into the incident ticket immediately. This is the audit trail for the post-mortem.
The on-call automation tool queries these dimensions from Prometheus and emits a severity recommendation. The engineer makes the final call.

Error rate and latency cannot detect a degraded model that still returns 200 OK with plausible-looking but incorrect completions. A separate quality signal fills this gap by running a lightweight automated evaluator against a sample of live completions.
Compare the rolling score against a 7-day baseline. A drop > 10 % below baseline triggers a P0 content-quality incident; 5-10 % below is P1. Emit these signals as Prometheus gauges so
can include them without changing the function signature.
Concrete examples prevent miscalibration during high-stress pages.
Quality score drops 15 % — quality dimension drives P0, but no confirmed safety signal, quality gate triggers P2 after 5 min
Error rate 1-5 %; latency 1.1x-1.5x; single-digit customer scope
No customer impact; monitoring cosmetic issue
Severity Drift — Upgrading and Downgrading Mid-Incident
An incident's severity must be reassessed whenever new information arrives.
Safety signal confirmed during P2 latency incident
Error rate drops below P1 threshold after mitigation
Release secondary on-call; keep war-room open
Customer scope grows as traffic shifts between regions
Page primary on-call; open war-room bridge
Investigation shows false-positive in safety pipeline
Confirm with safety team lead before downgrading
Affected cohort identified as a single tenant
Notify tenant; close org-wide status page update
Never downgrade a safety or privacy incident without sign-off from the safety team lead or privacy officer.

groups:
  - name: claude_severity
    interval: 60s
    rules:

      - alert: CriticalErrorRate
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) * 100 > 25
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: P0
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "API error rate > 25% - P0 incident"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/claude/p0-error-rate"

      - alert: HighErrorRate
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) * 100 > 5
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: P1
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "API error rate 5-25% - P1 incident"

      - alert: HighLatencyRatio
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(claude_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
          / clamp_min(claude_latency_baseline_p50, 0.001) > 2
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: P1
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "P50 latency > 2x baseline - P1 incident"

      - alert: ContentQualityDrop
        expr: claude_completion_quality_score < claude_quality_baseline * 0.90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: P0
          team: model-health
        annotations:
          summary: "Completion quality > 10% below baseline"
After every P0 or P1 incident, the post-mortem must include a severity calibration section answering three questions:
If the auto-assessment proposed P1 but engineers escalated to P0 within 10 minutes, the thresholds may be too lenient.
How long did severity drift take?
If the incident spent more than 30 minutes at the wrong severity, identify the measurement gap and add a new signal or lower the reassessment interval.
Audit whether every escalation step fired and whether any paged engineer was unnecessary.

Feed calibration findings back into the thresholds table and Prometheus alert rules as a quarterly tuning exercise. New model variants and enterprise traffic growth shift baselines and require threshold adjustments.

Claude's severity model extends the standard availability/latency rubric with safety, privacy, and content-quality dimensions that must trigger P0 on any confirmed case — overriding all quantitative thresholds. The five levels (P0-P4) map deterministically to paging policies, status-page updates, and executive briefs.
P0 triggers on safety/privacy violation or > 25 % error rate — any single dimension suffices
assess_severity() returns the worst severity across all quantitative dimensions
Safety and privacy signals are set by webhook; they always win over computed thresholds
The classification decision flow has six ordered steps — stop at the first severity match
Content quality scoring catches degraded model serving that error-rate dashboards miss
Severity drift is expected — reassess on every new data point and record the rationale
Post-mortem calibration reviews keep thresholds aligned with evolving traffic profiles
